preamble.summary: God continues speaking from the whirlwind, but the subject shifts from cosmology to zoology. Chapter 38 asked Job about the architecture of the earth, the behavior of weather, and the laws of the stars. Now God asks about animals — creatures that live beyond human control and often beyond human comprehension. The parade of animals is deliberately chosen: mountain goats who give birth on cliffs no human visits, the wild donkey who despises the city, the wild ox who will not serve at Job's manger, the ostrich who abandons her eggs yet runs faster than the horse, the war horse who laughs at fear and charges into battle, and the hawk and eagle who soar by wisdom Job did not give them. Each animal embodies freedom, wildness, strangeness, or power that exists entirely outside the human economy. God did not make these creatures for Job. They serve no human purpose. They are magnificent on their own terms, and God delights in them.
preamble.remarkable: The animal portraits in Job 39 are among the finest nature poetry in any language. They are not illustrations of theological propositions — they are celebrations of creaturely existence in its own right. The wild donkey is not a moral lesson; it is a wild donkey, free and fierce and contemptuous of civilization. The war horse does not symbolize courage; it is courage made flesh, snorting, pawing, laughing at fear. The ostrich is not a parable about foolish parenting; she is a bizarre, beautiful anomaly — cruel to her young by human standards, yet equipped with speed no horse can match. God does not explain these creatures; God presents them. The theological effect is not a lesson but a reorientation: Job has been asking why the universe does not conform to human categories of justice. God's answer is to show him a universe that does not conform to human categories at all. The wild donkey does not care about Job's lawsuit. The eagle does not know about retribution theology. The world is bigger, wilder, stranger, and more glorious than any system — whether the friends' or Job's — can contain.
preamble.friction: The central tension in this chapter is the ostrich passage (verses 13-18), which contains the most explicit divine commentary in the speech: God says He did not give the ostrich wisdom but gave her speed instead. This is the only place in the speech where God explains one of the creatures rather than simply presenting it. Some scholars consider it an interpolation because it breaks the pattern of questions — God makes statements here rather than asking. But it may be deliberately placed as the center of the animal catalog, the one creature that most clearly defies human categories: she is cruel, foolish, and glorious all at once. The theological problem is sharp: if God withheld wisdom from the ostrich, does God withhold understanding from humans too? Does the speech imply that Job's incomprehension is by divine design?
preamble.connections: The animal catalog connects to Psalm 104, which also celebrates wild creatures (wild donkeys, rock badgers, lions) as evidence of God's wisdom and delight. The war horse passage (verses 19-25) has no close parallel in Scripture but echoes ancient Near Eastern horse literature. The hawk and eagle at the end (verses 26-30) connect to Isaiah 40:31 ('those who wait for YHWH shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings like eagles'). The entire chapter anticipates the Behemoth and Leviathan passages in chapters 40-41, where God will present the two supreme animals. The movement from domestic concerns (chapters 3-37) to wild animals is a geographic movement as well — from the inhabited world to the wilderness, the desert, the cliff face, the sky. God is pulling Job's attention away from the human world and toward the larger creation.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲיָדַ֗עְתָּ עֵ֭ת לֶ֣דֶת יַעֲלֵי־סָ֑לַע תְּחוֹלֵ֖ל אַיָּל֣וֹת תִּשְׁמֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Knowest thou the time when the wild goats of the rock bring forth? or canst thou mark when the hinds do calve?
verses.rendering:
Do you know when the mountain goats give birth on the cliff?
Do you watch over the deer as they labor?
verses.expanded_rendering: The animal procession begins with creatures that give birth in places no human can reach. Hayadata et ledet ya'alei sala ('do you know the time of the birthing of the cliff-goats') — the ya'alei sala are ibex or wild goats that live on sheer rock faces. They give birth on ledges hundreds of feet above the ground, and no human midwife attends. God knows the time; Job does not. The techolel ayyalot tishmor ('do you watch over the deer/hinds as they writhe in labor') extends the question: the ayyalot (female deer) give birth in hidden places, and their labor is a mystery observed only by God. The opening move of the animal catalog establishes the principle: the wild world reproduces itself without human knowledge or assistance.
translator_notes: The ya'alei sala ('rock-goats, ibex') are mentioned also in 1 Samuel 24:2 and Psalm 104:18. They inhabit the most inaccessible terrain in the Near East — the cliffs of Ein Gedi and the Judean wilderness. The verb cholel ('to writhe, to be in labor') describes the pain of animal childbirth, observed by God alone.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּסְפֹּ֣ר יְרָחִ֣ים תְּמַלֶּ֑אנָה וְ֝יָדַ֗עְתָּ עֵ֣ת לִדְתָּֽנָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Canst thou number the months that they fulfil? or knowest thou the time when they bring forth?
verses.rendering:
Can you count the months until they are due?
Do you know the time of their delivery?
translator_notes: Tispor yerachim temalle'nah ('can you count the months they fulfill') — each species has its own gestation period, its own biological calendar. God tracks each pregnancy. The veyadata et lidtanah ('and you know the time of their bearing') challenges Job to demonstrate reproductive knowledge he does not possess. The verse implies that God functions as the midwife of the wild — counting months, watching over pregnancies, attending births no human sees.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּ֭כְרַעְנָה יַלְדֵיהֶ֣ן תְּפַלַּ֑חְנָה חֶבְלֵיהֶ֥ם תְּשַׁלַּֽחְנָה׃
verses.text_kjv: They bow themselves, they bring forth their young ones, they cast out their sorrows.
verses.rendering:
They crouch down, push out their young,
and are done with their birth pangs.
translator_notes: Tikhrana ('they bow down, they crouch') yaldeihen tefallechna ('their young they push out') — the description is vivid and physical: the animals crouch and deliver. The chevleihem teshallechna ('their labor-pains they send away') — once the birth is complete, the pain is over. The word chevel ('labor-pain, rope, cord') is the same word used for human labor pains throughout Scripture. These wild animals experience pain, endure it, and move on — without explanation, without protest, without asking why.

verses.text_hebrew: יַחְלְמ֣וּ בְ֭נֵיהֶם יִרְבּ֣וּ בַבָּ֑ר יָ֝צְא֗וּ וְלֹא־שָׁ֥בוּ לָֽמוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Their young ones are in good liking, they grow up with corn; they go forth, and return not unto them.
verses.rendering:
Their young grow strong, they thrive in the open;
they leave and do not return.
translator_notes: Yachlemu beneihem ('their children become strong/healthy') yirbu va-bar ('they increase in the open field'). The yatse'u velo shavu lamo ('they go out and do not return to them') describes the natural weaning process: the young grow up, leave, and never come back. There is no sentimentality here — this is how wild animals live. The theological point is understated but clear: God sustains a world in which creatures are born, grow strong, leave their parents, and live independently. The system works without human management.

verses.text_hebrew: מִֽי־שִׁלַּ֣ח פֶּ֣רֶא חׇפְשִׁ֑י וּמֹסְר֥וֹת עָ֝ר֗וֹד מִ֣י פִתֵּֽחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: Who hath sent out the wild ass free? or who hath loosed the bands of the wild ass?
verses.rendering:
Who set the wild donkey free?
Who untied the ropes of the onager?
verses.expanded_rendering: The wild donkey (pere) is the symbol of untamable freedom. Mi shillach pere chofshi ('who sent the wild donkey out free') — the word chofshi ('free') is the legal term for a freed slave (Exodus 21:2). God emancipated the wild donkey. It was never domesticated because God declared it free. The parallel umoserot arod mi fitte'ach ('and the bonds of the arod/wild donkey, who opened them') uses arod, a synonym for the wild donkey, and moserot ('bonds, fetters') — someone removed the chains. The implied answer is God. God made an animal whose entire identity is freedom, and God delights in that freedom even though it serves no human purpose.
translator_notes: The pere (wild donkey, onager) is one of the most frequently mentioned wild animals in the Hebrew Bible (Genesis 16:12, Isaiah 32:14, Jeremiah 2:24, Hosea 8:9). It symbolizes wildness, independence, and refusal to be domesticated. Ishmael is compared to a pere adam ('wild donkey of a man') in Genesis 16:12. God's delight in the wild donkey's freedom is a direct challenge to the anthropocentric view that all creatures exist to serve humanity.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשֶׁר־שַׂ֣מְתִּי עֲרָבָ֣ה בֵית֑וֹ וּמִשְׁכְּנוֹתָ֖יו מְלֵחָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Whose house I have made the wilderness, and the barren land his dwellings.
verses.rendering:
I made the wasteland its home
and the salt flats its dwelling.
translator_notes: Asher samti aravah beito ('which I set the steppe/wilderness as its house') — God assigned the wild donkey its habitat, and that habitat is the aravah ('desert plain, steppe, wilderness'). The umishkenotav melechah ('and its dwellings the salt land') — the melechah is salt-crusted terrain where nothing grows. The wild donkey lives in places no sane farmer would settle. Its home is the anti-city, the anti-garden. God gave it this home deliberately, and the wild donkey thrives there.

verses.text_hebrew: יִשְׂחַ֣ק לַהֲמ֣וֹן קִרְיָ֑ה תְּשֻׁא֥וֹת נ֝וֹגֵ֗שׂ לֹ֣א יִשְׁמָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: He scorneth the multitude of the city, neither regardeth he the crying of the driver.
verses.rendering:
It laughs at the noise of the city.
It does not hear the driver's shouts.
translator_notes: Yischaq lahamon qiryah ('it laughs at the tumult of the city') — the verb sachaq ('to laugh, to mock') expresses contempt. The wild donkey finds the city ridiculous. The teshu'ot noges lo yishma ('the shouts of the driver it does not hear') — the noges ('driver, taskmaster, oppressor') is the same word used for the Egyptian slave-drivers in Exodus. The wild donkey is immune to the taskmaster. It cannot be driven, whipped, or controlled. It lives outside every system of domination. God made a creature that is constitutionally free.

verses.text_hebrew: יְתוּר הָרִ֣ים מִרְעֵ֑הוּ וְאַחַ֖ר כׇּל־יָר֣וֹק יִדְרֽוֹשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: The range of the mountains is his pasture, and he searcheth after every green thing.
verses.rendering:
It roams the mountains as its pasture
and searches for every green thing.
translator_notes: Yetur harim mir'ehu ('the range of the mountains is its pasture') — the wild donkey's grazing land is the entire mountain range. No fence contains it. The ve'achar kol yaroq yidrosh ('and after every green thing it searches') — the verb darash ('to seek, to search') is the same verb used for seeking God. The wild donkey searches for food with the same intensity a worshipper searches for God. The portrait is complete: the wild donkey is free, wild, self-sufficient, contemptuous of civilization, and wholly sustained by God.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲיֹ֣אבֶה רֵּ֣ים עׇבְדֶ֑ךָ אִם־יָ֝לִ֗ין עַל־אֲבוּסֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Will the unicorn be willing to serve thee, or abide by thy crib?
verses.rendering:
Will the wild ox consent to serve you?
Will it spend the night at your feeding trough?
verses.expanded_rendering: The re'em (wild ox, aurochs) was the largest and most powerful bovine in the ancient Near East — now extinct, it was a massive creature that could not be domesticated. Hayo'veh re'em ovdekha ('will the wild ox be willing to be your servant') — the verb avah ('to be willing, to consent') implies that the wild ox has a will, and that will is to refuse. The im yalin al avusekha ('or will it lodge at your feeding trough') asks whether this enormous beast will sleep in Job's barn like a docile cow. The answer is obviously no. The wild ox exists on its own terms, and no human can change those terms.
translator_notes: The re'em (traditionally translated 'unicorn' in the KJV, following the LXX monoceros) is almost certainly the aurochs (Bos primigenius), the wild ancestor of domestic cattle, which stood six feet at the shoulder and had enormous horns. It went extinct in 1627. The re'em appears in Numbers 23:22, Deuteronomy 33:17, Psalm 22:21, and Psalm 92:10, always as a symbol of overwhelming strength. God made an animal too powerful for human service.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲתִקְשׇׁר־רֵ֭ים בְּתֶ֣לֶם עֲבֹת֑וֹ אִם־יְשַׂדֵּ֖ד עֲמָקִ֣ים אַחֲרֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Canst thou bind the unicorn with his band in the furrow? or will he harrow the valleys after thee?
verses.rendering:
Can you bind the wild ox to a furrow with ropes?
Will it harrow the valleys behind you?
translator_notes: Hatiqshor re'em betelem avoto ('can you bind the wild ox in the furrow with its rope') — can Job yoke the wild ox to a plow and make it walk a straight furrow? The im yesadded amaqim acharekha ('will it harrow the valleys after you') — will it follow behind Job, breaking up the soil? The wild ox's strength would be extraordinary in agriculture, but it cannot be harnessed. Power without submission — God made that too.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲתִבְטַח־בּ֭וֹ כִּי־רַ֣ב כֹּח֑וֹ וְתַעֲזֹ֖ב אֵלָ֣יו יְגִיעֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Wilt thou trust him, because his strength is great? or wilt thou leave thy labour to him?
verses.rendering:
Can you trust it because its strength is so great?
Would you leave your heavy work to it?
translator_notes: Hativtach bo ki rav kocho ('will you trust in it because its strength is great') — the wild ox is immensely strong, but strength without obedience is useless to a farmer. Trust requires reliability, and the wild ox is reliably unreliable. The veta'azov elav yegi'ekha ('and will you leave your toil to it') — yegi'a ('toil, labor, the fruit of labor') is what Job has worked for. Would he entrust his harvest to an animal that will not obey? The verse tests whether raw power is the same as dependable service. It is not.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲתַאֲמִ֣ין בּ֭וֹ כִּי־יָשִׁ֣יב זַרְעֶ֑ךָ וְ֝גׇרְנְךָ֗ יֶאֱסֹֽף׃
verses.text_kjv: Wilt thou believe him, that he will bring home thy seed, and gather it into thy barn?
verses.rendering:
Do you trust it to bring in your grain
and gather it to your threshing floor?
translator_notes: Hata'amin bo ki yashiv zar'ekha ('will you believe in it that it will return your seed/grain') — the verb he'emin ('to trust, to believe') is the same root as emunah ('faithfulness') and amen. Can Job have faith in the wild ox? The vegornekha ye'esof ('and your threshing floor it will gather') asks whether the ox will complete the harvest. The answer is no — the wild ox has no interest in human agriculture. Its magnificence is not for human use. God made beauty and power that serve no one but the Creator.

verses.text_hebrew: כְּנַף־רְנָנִ֥ים נֶעֱלָ֑סָה אִם־אֶ֝בְרָ֗ה חֲסִידָ֥ה וְנֹצָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Gavest thou the goodly wings unto the peacocks? or wings and feathers unto the ostrich?
verses.rendering:
The wings of the ostrich flap joyfully —
but are they the pinions of the stork?
verses.expanded_rendering: The ostrich passage begins with a riddle. Kenaf renanim ne'elasah ('the wing of the ostriches flaps joyfully') — the renanim ('ostriches,' literally 'shriekers' or 'joyful ones') flap their wings with exuberance, but those wings cannot achieve flight. The im evrah chasidah venotsah ('are they the pinion of the stork and feathers') — the chasidah ('stork,' literally 'the faithful/devoted one') has true flight feathers. The ostrich has wings but not flight. The stork has both. The comparison is not a criticism — it is a mystery. God made a bird that rejoices in wings that do not work for their apparent purpose. The ostrich is a creature that defies categories: a bird that cannot fly, a parent that abandons its eggs, an animal without wisdom that can outrun a horse. God presents the ostrich as evidence that creation contains deliberate anomalies.
translator_notes: The word renanim (from ranan, 'to shout with joy, to sing') names the ostrich for its cry. The chasidah ('stork') is named for chesed ('faithful love, devotion') — ironically, the stork is the devoted parent while the ostrich is the negligent one. The juxtaposition of names highlights the contrast: the 'joyful singer' does not fly; the 'faithful one' does.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־תַעֲזֹ֣ב לָאָ֣רֶץ בֵּצֶ֑יהָ וְעַל־עָפָ֥ר תְּחַמֵּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Which leaveth her eggs in the earth, and warmeth them in dust,
verses.rendering:
She leaves her eggs on the ground
and lets them warm in the dust,
translator_notes: Ki ta'azov la'arets betseiah ('for she abandons to the earth her eggs') — the verb azav ('to leave, to abandon, to forsake') is strong. The ostrich does not carefully nest her eggs; she leaves them on the ground. The ve'al afar techamem ('and upon dust she warms them') — the eggs are incubated by the heat of the desert floor, not by attentive sitting. By human standards, this is negligent parenting. By divine design, it works — ostrich eggs are remarkably heat-resistant and the species has survived for millions of years.

verses.text_hebrew: וַתִּשְׁכַּ֗ח כִּי־רֶ֣גֶל תְּזוּרֶ֑הָ וְחַיַּ֖ת הַשָּׂדֶ֣ה תְּדוּשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And forgetteth that the foot may crush them, or that the wild beast may break them.
verses.rendering:
forgetting that a foot may crush them
or a wild animal may trample them.
translator_notes: Vatishkach ki regel tezureha ('and she forgets that a foot may crush it') — the ostrich does not guard against the most obvious danger. The vechayyat ha-sadeh tedusheha ('and the animal of the field may trample it') — any passing beast could destroy her eggs. The ostrich's apparent carelessness is presented without moral judgment. God made her this way. Not every creature parents the way humans think creatures should.

verses.text_hebrew: הִקְשִׁ֣יחַ בָּנֶ֣יהָ לְּלֹא־לָ֑הּ לְרִ֖יק יְגִיעָ֣הּ בְּלִי־פָֽחַד׃
verses.text_kjv: She is hardened against her young ones, as though they were not hers: her labour is in vain without fear;
verses.rendering:
She treats her young harshly, as if not her own.
Her labor may be for nothing — she does not care.
translator_notes: Hiqshiach baneiha lelo lah ('she hardens against her children as if not hers') — the verb hiqshiach ('to harden, to treat cruelly') suggests the ostrich is indifferent to her own offspring. The leriq yegi'ah beli fachad ('her toil is for emptiness, without fear') — her labor may produce nothing, and she feels no anxiety about it. The phrase beli fachad ('without fear') is key: the ostrich does not fear failure. She does not worry. This is not wisdom — the text will say God withheld wisdom from her — but it is a kind of freedom. She exists without the burden of anxious care that torments Job.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־הִשָּׁ֣הּ אֱל֣וֹהַ חׇכְמָ֑ה וְלֹא־חָ֥לַק לָ֝֗הּ בַּבִּינָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Because God hath deprived her of wisdom, neither hath he imparted to her understanding.
verses.rendering:
For God did not grant her wisdom
or give her a share of understanding.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the only verse in the divine speech where God explains a creature's behavior rather than simply presenting it. Ki hishshah Eloah chokhmah ('for God caused her to forget wisdom') — the verb nashah ('to forget, to deprive of memory') means God deliberately withheld wisdom from the ostrich. She is not stupid by accident; she is unwise by design. The velo chalaq lah ba-bvinah ('and He did not apportion to her understanding') confirms it: God distributes wisdom selectively. Not every creature gets the same allotment. The theological implications are staggering — if God withholds wisdom from creatures by design, then the ostrich's foolishness is part of the plan. She is meant to be what she is. And if God distributes wisdom unevenly in the animal kingdom, perhaps God distributes understanding unevenly among humans too — not as punishment but as design.
translator_notes: This verse breaks the speech's question-only pattern — God makes a declarative statement about the ostrich. Some scholars consider this evidence that the ostrich section is a later addition. Others argue it is the deliberate center of the animal catalog, the point where God most directly addresses the question of why creatures (and by extension, humans) lack the understanding they wish they had. The answer is discomfiting: because God chose not to give it.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּ֭עֵת בַּמָּר֣וֹם תַּמְרִ֑יא תִּשְׂחַ֖ק לַסּ֣וּס וּלְרֹכְבֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: What time she lifteth up herself on high, she scorneth the horse and his rider.
verses.rendering:
But when she spreads her wings and runs,
she laughs at the horse and its rider.
verses.expanded_rendering: The ostrich portrait ends with a stunning reversal. Ka'et ba-marom tamri ('at the time she lifts herself on high') — when the ostrich runs, she lifts her useless wings and achieves something better than flight. She becomes the fastest creature on the ground. Tischaq la-sus ulerokhevo ('she laughs at the horse and its rider') — the verb sachaq ('to laugh, to mock') is the same word used for the wild donkey laughing at the city in verse 7. The ostrich, who cannot fly, who abandons her eggs, who has no wisdom — this same ostrich can outrun a war horse with an armed rider on its back. God withheld wisdom but gave speed. God withheld flight but gave glory. The ostrich is not a failure; she is a different kind of triumph. The theological point pierces to the heart of Job's complaint: what looks like deprivation may be a different form of endowment.
translator_notes: Ostriches can run at speeds exceeding 40 miles per hour, faster than any horse. The ancient world knew this and marveled at it. The portrait of the ostrich is a masterpiece of reversal: every apparent deficiency (flightless wings, abandoned eggs, no wisdom) is answered by an unexpected gift (speed, survival, fearlessness). The creature that seems most cursed is in some ways the most free.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲתִתֵּ֣ן לַסּ֣וּס גְּבוּרָ֑ה הֲתַלְבִּ֖ישׁ צַוָּאר֣וֹ רַעְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Hast thou given the horse strength? hast thou clothed his neck with thunder?
verses.rendering:
Did you give the horse its strength?
Did you clothe its neck with a flowing mane?
verses.expanded_rendering: The war horse enters the poem, and the poetry itself seems to accelerate. Hatitten la-sus gevurah ('did you give the horse strength') — the gevurah ('might, power, warrior-strength') is God's gift, not a product of breeding or training. The hatalbbish tsavvaro ra'mah ('did you clothe its neck with ra'mah') — the word ra'mah is debated: it may mean 'thunder' (from ra'am), suggesting the horse's neck carries the force of a thunderstorm, or 'mane' (from a related root), referring to the flowing hair that makes the horse's neck so majestic. Either reading works: the horse's neck is either dressed in thunder or in glory. God is the one who dressed it.
translator_notes: The war horse passage (verses 19-25) is the longest and most vivid animal portrait in the speech. The horse is the only domesticated animal in God's catalog, but it is presented not as a servant but as a creature of terrifying power and joy. The ra'mah ('thunder/mane') debate is ancient — the LXX reads 'fear' (phobos), while the Vulgate reads 'neighing' (hinnitum). The ambiguity may be intentional: the horse's mane is like thunder.

verses.text_hebrew: הְ‍ֽתַרְעִישֶׁ֥נּוּ כָאַרְבֶּ֑ה ה֖וֹד נַחְר֣וֹ אֵימָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Canst thou make him afraid as a grasshopper? the glory of his nostrils is terrible.
verses.rendering:
Can you make it leap like a locust?
The splendor of its snorting is terrifying.
translator_notes: Hatar'ishennu ka'arbeh ('can you make it tremble/leap like a locust') — the comparison to a locust may seem odd, but it captures the horse's explosive forward movement, the way it springs into a gallop. The hod nachro eimah ('the glory/majesty of its snorting is terror') — the horse's snort (nachar) has hod ('majesty, splendor'), the same word used for God's glory in Psalm 8:1 and 104:1. The horse's nostrils exhale something majestic. Terror and beauty merge.

verses.text_hebrew: יַחְפְּר֣וּ בָ֭עֵמֶק וְיָשִׂ֣ישׂ בְּכֹ֑חַ יֵ֝צֵ֗א לִקְרַאת־נָֽשֶׁק׃
verses.text_kjv: He paweth in the valley, and rejoiceth in his strength: he goeth on to meet the armed men.
verses.rendering:
It paws in the valley and rejoices in its strength.
It charges out to meet the weapons.
translator_notes: Yachperu va'emeq ('they paw/dig in the valley') — the war horse paws the ground before battle, impatient to charge. The veyasis bekoach ('and it rejoices in strength') — the horse experiences joy in its own power. This is not mere instinct; it is delight. The yetse liqrat nasheq ('it goes out to meet the weaponry/armor') — the horse moves toward danger, not away from it. It runs toward swords. The portrait inverts every human instinct of self-preservation.

verses.text_hebrew: יִשְׂחַ֣ק לְ֭פַחַד וְלֹ֣א יֵחָ֑ת וְלֹא־יָ֝שׁ֗וּב מִפְּנֵי־חָֽרֶב׃
verses.text_kjv: He mocketh at fear, and is not affrighted; neither turneth he back from the sword.
verses.rendering:
It laughs at fear and is not dismayed.
It does not turn back from the sword.
translator_notes: Yischaq lefachad ('it laughs at fear') — for the third time in this chapter, a creature laughs (the wild donkey at the city, the ostrich at the horse, now the horse at fear itself). The velo yechat ('and is not shattered/dismayed') — the verb chatat ('to be shattered, to be terrified') describes the kind of fear that breaks a person. The horse does not experience it. The velo yashuv mippenei charev ('and it does not turn from the face of the sword') — the horse faces the blade and does not flinch. This is the ultimate portrait of courage — not the absence of danger but the refusal to retreat from it.

verses.text_hebrew: עָ֭לָיו תִּרְנֶ֣ה אַשְׁפָּ֑ה לַ֖הַב חֲנִ֣ית וְכִידֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: The quiver rattleth against him, the glittering spear and the shield.
verses.rendering:
The quiver rattles against its side,
the flashing spear and the javelin.
translator_notes: Alav tirneh ashpah ('upon it the quiver rings/rattles') — the sound of arrows jangling in their case against the horse's flank as it gallops. The lahav chanit vekhidon ('the flame of the spear and the javelin') — lahav ('flame, blade') describes the glint of metal, the flash of weapon-light. The verse is pure sensory description: sound (the rattling quiver) and sight (the flashing spear). The horse is surrounded by implements of death and runs faster.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּרַ֣עַשׁ וְ֭רֹגֶז יְגַמֶּא־אָ֑רֶץ וְלֹ֥א יַ֝אֲמִ֗ין כִּי־ק֥וֹל שׁוֹפָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: He swalloweth the ground with fierceness and rage: neither believeth he that it is the sound of the trumpet.
verses.rendering:
With trembling and fury it devours the ground.
It cannot stand still when the trumpet sounds.
verses.expanded_rendering: Bera'ash verogez yegamme arets ('with shaking and rage it swallows the earth') — the horse's gallop is so violent it seems to consume the ground beneath it. The verb gama ('to swallow, to drink') applied to the earth is hyperbolic: the horse drinks the distance, eats up the terrain. The velo ya'amin ki qol shofar ('and it does not believe/stand firm at the sound of the ram's horn') — when the battle trumpet sounds, the horse cannot contain itself. The verb he'emin here may mean 'to stand still, to be steady' rather than 'to believe.' The trumpet transforms the horse from a waiting animal into an unstoppable force. The shofar — used in Israelite worship and warfare alike — triggers the horse's deepest instinct.
translator_notes: The verb gama ('to swallow') applied to the earth is one of the most powerful images in the passage — the horse literally eats distance, consuming ground as it charges. The shofar (ram's horn trumpet) connects this military scene to Israel's liturgical life — the same instrument that calls Israel to worship calls the horse to war.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּדֵ֤י שֹׁפָ֨ר ׀ יֹ֘אמַ֤ר הֶאָ֗ח וּ֭מֵרָחוֹק יָרִ֣יחַ מִלְחָמָ֑ה רַ֖עַם שָׂרִ֣ים וּתְרוּעָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He saith among the trumpets, Ha, ha; and he smelleth the battle afar off, the thunder of the captains, and the shouting.
verses.rendering:
At each blast of the trumpet it cries, 'Ha!'
From far off it catches the scent of battle —
the thunder of commanders and the war cry.
verses.expanded_rendering: The war horse portrait reaches its climax. Bedei shofar yomar he'ach ('at the sufficiency of the trumpet it says Ha!') — the horse speaks. It cries out ha! — a shout of exultation, not a whinny but a battle cry. The ummerachoq yariach milchamah ('and from far off it smells war') — the horse's nostrils detect battle before it arrives. It smells war on the wind: the sweat, the blood, the metal, the fear. The ra'am sarim uteRu'ah ('the thunder of commanders and the shout') — the horse hears the roar of officers and the war cry of charging soldiers, and it responds with joy. This is the only animal in the Bible that speaks — ha! — and what it says is pure, wordless exhilaration. The portrait of the war horse is God's most elaborate answer to the question of wild, purposeful, fearless existence. The horse does not understand the war. It does not know why it charges. But it charges with its whole being, and its joy is complete.
translator_notes: The exclamation he'ach ('ha!') is an onomatopoeia — the horse's excited snort or neigh rendered as human speech. The fact that the horse 'says' something puts it in the company of creation's other speakers: the morning stars that sing (38:7), the lightning bolts that say 'here we are' (38:35), and the night that announced Job's conception (3:3). God's creation is full of voices, and the horse's voice is one of fierce delight.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲ֭מִבִּ֣ינָתְךָ֗ יַאֲבֶר־נֵ֑ץ יִפְרֹ֖שׂ כְּנָפָ֣יו לְתֵימָֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: Doth the hawk fly by thy wisdom, and stretch her wings toward the south?
verses.rendering:
Does the hawk soar by your wisdom,
spreading its wings toward the south?
translator_notes: Hamibbinatkha ya'aver nets ('is it from your understanding the hawk flies') — the nets ('hawk, falcon') flies by an understanding Job did not supply. The yifros kenafav leteiman ('it spreads its wings toward the south') — the migratory instinct that carries hawks southward in winter is a form of wisdom, but it is God's wisdom implanted in the bird, not knowledge the hawk learned or Job taught. Migration is a mystery of navigation that humans still do not fully understand.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־עַל־פִּ֭יךָ יַגְבִּ֣יהַ נָ֑שֶׁר וְ֝כִ֗י יָרִ֥ים קִנּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Doth the eagle mount up at thy command, and make her nest on high?
verses.rendering:
Does the eagle rise at your command
and build its nest on high?
translator_notes: Im al pikha yagbiha nasher ('is it at your mouth/command the eagle soars high') — the nasher ('eagle' or 'vulture,' likely the griffon vulture) does not take orders from Job. The vekhi yarim qinno ('and that it raises its nest') — the eagle nests at heights no human chooses. The word al pikha ('at your mouth') echoes the idea that creation obeys God's mouth/word (Genesis 1), not Job's.

verses.text_hebrew: סֶ֣לַע יִ֭שְׁכֹּן וְיִתְלֹנָ֑ן עַ֖ל שֶׁן־סֶ֣לַע וּמְצוּדָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: She dwelleth and abideth on the rock, upon the crag of the rock, and the strong place.
verses.rendering:
It dwells on the rock and lodges there,
on the crag of the cliff and the stronghold.
translator_notes: Sela yishkon veyitlonan ('on the rock it dwells and lodges') — the eagle's home is the sela ('rock, cliff'), the most inaccessible terrain. The al shen sela umetsudah ('on the tooth of the rock and the fortress') — shen sela ('tooth of the rock') describes a sharp, narrow pinnacle. The metsudah ('stronghold, fortress') is a military term applied to a bird's nest — the eagle lives in a natural fortress. Like the mountain goats in verse 1, the eagle inhabits places humans cannot reach. God's creatures have territories beyond human jurisdiction.

verses.text_hebrew: מִשָּׁ֣ם חָ֣פַר אֹ֑כֶל לְ֝מֵרָח֗וֹק עֵינָ֥יו יַבִּֽיטוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: From thence she seeketh the prey, and her eyes behold afar off.
verses.rendering:
From there it searches for food;
its eyes scan the distance.
translator_notes: Mishsham chafar okhel ('from there it searches for food') — the eagle hunts from its high perch, scanning the ground below. The lemerachoq einav yabbitu ('from afar its eyes gaze') — the eagle's vision is legendary. It sees prey from distances no human eye can match. The verse describes a divinely engineered predator: positioned high, equipped with extraordinary sight, designed to hunt. The eagle's vision is a gift from the same God who asks Job to see beyond his own suffering.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶפְרֹחָ֥יו יְעַלְעוּ־דָ֑ם וּבַאֲשֶׁ֖ר חֲלָלִ֣ים שָׁ֣ם הֽוּא׃
verses.text_kjv: Her young ones also suck up blood: and where the slain are, there is she.
verses.rendering:
Its young ones feast on blood,
and where the slain are — there it is.
verses.expanded_rendering: The animal catalog ends with blood. Ve'efrochav ye'al'u dam ('and its chicks lap up blood') — the eagle feeds its young on the blood of prey. The image is not sanitized: this is a carnivorous world, and God made it that way. The uva'asher chalalim sham hu ('and where the slain are, there it is') — wherever there are corpses, the eagle appears. The word chalalim ('slain, pierced, casualties') is a battlefield term — the eagle is drawn to the aftermath of war, to death, to carrion. The chapter that began with the birth of mountain goat kids on a cliff ends with eagle chicks drinking blood from corpses. This is God's world: birth and death, tenderness and violence, beauty and terror, all held together by a wisdom Job has never possessed and cannot now deny. The animals do not argue about justice. They live, hunt, breed, and die within a system they did not design and do not question. God offers them to Job not as moral lessons but as evidence of a creation that exceeds every category Job has tried to impose upon it.
translator_notes: The final image — eagle chicks feeding on blood, the eagle present wherever the slain lie — connects to Jesus' words in Matthew 24:28 and Luke 17:37: 'Where the corpse is, there the vultures will gather.' The saying may have been proverbial even before Jesus, rooted in this verse. The chapter ends without resolution, without moral, without comfort — only with the stark reality of a world God made and governs on terms that are not humanity's to set.
